coredata.dc:title: Biocompatible bright orange emissive carbon dots: Multifunctional nanoprobes for highly specific sensing toxic Cr(VI) ions and mitochondrial targeting cancer cell imaging

coredata.dc:description: Although several luminescent-based nanostructured materials are used as cellular imaging probes, creating a biocompatible subcellular imaging probe can be challenging. Instantaneously, it is crucial and urgently needed for certain fluorescent nanoprobes to identify possibly harmful heavy metal ions. We present a straightforward one-pot preparation of bright orange emissive (quantum yield approximately 16 %) Nitrogen/Sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N/S CDs) from citric acid and methylene blue as raw materials that will serve as a specific Cr(VI) ions sensor and an effective mitochondrial labeling in cancer cells. They had several benefits including low ecological impact, facile synthesis, good water solubility, photostability, and high stability. We found that N/S CDs photoluminescence (PL) could be reduced when Cr(VI) ions were present near them, and the PL reduction occurred highly sensitively to the presence of Cr(VI) compared to other metal ions including Cr(III) ions. This specific reduction of PL was due to the non-fluorescent complex formation through the inner filter effect (IFE). The established fluorescence-based sensing technique could serve for Cr(VI) ion quantification with exceptional sensing efficiency in the wide linear range of 7 to 70 μM (R2 = 0.9873), with the limit of detection of 53.5 nM. It was also revealed that the current fluorescent probe could be encouragingly utilized to quantify the concentration of Cr(VI) ions in various water specimens such as tap water. In addition, they were shown to function as a mitochondria-targeting nanoprobe in human cancer cells (ME 180 cells and Hela cells) for cell imaging. Concludingly, it was envisioned that these fluorescent nanoprobes could find a use in real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) ions in water-based ecosystems with ultrahigh sensitivity and cell image tracking via mitochondria labeling as biocompatible nanoprobes.

head.ce:title: Biocompatible bright orange emissive carbon dots: Multifunctional nanoprobes for highly specific sensing toxic Cr(VI) ions and mitochondrial targeting cancer cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although several luminescent-based nanostructured materials are used as cellular imaging probes, creating a biocompatible subcellular imaging probe can be challenging. Instantaneously, it is crucial and urgently needed for certain fluorescent nanoprobes to identify possibly harmful heavy metal ions. We present a straightforward one-pot preparation of bright orange emissive (quantum yield approximately 16 %) Nitrogen/Sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N/S CDs) from citric acid and methylene blue as raw materials that will serve as a specific Cr(VI) ions sensor and an effective mitochondrial labeling in cancer cells. They had several benefits including low ecological impact, facile synthesis, good water solubility, photostability, and high stability. We found that N/S CDs photoluminescence (PL) could be reduced when Cr(VI) ions were present near them, and the PL reduction occurred highly sensitively to the presence of Cr(VI) compared to other metal ions including Cr(III) ions. This specific reduction of PL was due to the non-fluorescent complex formation through the inner filter effect (IFE). The established fluorescence-based sensing technique could serve for Cr(VI) ion quantification with exceptional sensing efficiency in the wide linear range of 7 to 70 μM (R2 = 0.9873), with the limit of detection of 53.5 nM. It was also revealed that the current fluorescent probe could be encouragingly utilized to quantify the concentration of Cr(VI) ions in various water specimens such as tap water. In addition, they were shown to function as a mitochondria-targeting nanoprobe in human cancer cells (ME 180 cells and Hela cells) for cell imaging. Concludingly, it was envisioned that these fluorescent nanoprobes could find a use in real-time monitoring of Cr(VI) ions in water-based ecosystems with ultrahigh sensitivity and cell image tracking via mitochondria labeling as biocompatible nanoprobes.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination has been considered a grave problem in an ecosystem, demanding serious attention from not only scientific research fields but also government sectors [1,2]. Heavy metal contamination has brought risky issues to public well-being and the natural environment as modern industries develop rapidly. Among various heavy metals, hexavalent chromium (Cr(VI)) known to have the six oxidation states is critically harmful to public health and the ecosystem [1,3]. Chromium is widely utilized in the manufacture of paint, metallurgy, paper, and chemical compounds. Particularly when too much oxygen is present in the atmosphere, Cr(VI) oxidizes into the highly poisonous water-soluble oxidation state [4,5]. Accordingly, wastewater containing Cr(VI) from these industries has been the main source of Cr(VI) contamination in natural water bodies. Hexavalent (Cr (VI)), in contrast to other oxidation states such as Cr(0) and Cr(III), is deadly poisonous, mutagenetic, and carcinogenic to living things [6–8]. As Cr(VI) is easily soluble in an aquatic environment, its greater mobility and bio-accessibility tend to ramp up exposure to serious toxicity. Surprisingly, Cr(VI) can simply be infused into the cell membrane via a non-specific sulfate and phosphate anion transport pathway, and the subsequently formed reactive intermediates such as oxygen radicals, Cr(IV), and Cr(V), may cause DNA alteration [9,10]. Furthermore, Cr(VI) is difficult to detoxify physiologically and chemically, making it widely accessible to ecosystems. The major health problems caused by Cr(VI) include hemolytic anemia, skin allergies, liver cirrhosis, and so on [11,12]. Due to the toxicity of Cr(VI), the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) has categorized Cr(VI) as a class A carcinogen [13], and the World Health Organization (WHO) regulated the maximum tolerable limit of 10 μM Cr(VI) in drinking water [1,14]. This has continued to elevate the necessity for specific and ultrasensitive sensing of Cr (VI) ions for preemptive protection of health and the ecosystem.

Numerous systematic strategies, including chromatography [15], spectrofluorimetry [1,6], atomic absorption spectrometry [16], X-ray fluorescence [17], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [18] have been successfully implemented during the past two decades for the detection of Cr(VI) in various specimens. However, their sophisticated high-cost equipment and labor-intensive complex sample processing prevent most of these strategies from being practically used for in-situ real-time detection. Meanwhile, fluorescence-based optical sensors are considered a preferable alternative due to their high sensitivity, operation simplicity, and quick detection [19–22]. In addition, target analytes can be recognized exactly without sacrificing sensitivity when fluorophores are treated properly.

In the recent past, Water-dispersible CdTe quantum dots were used as fluorescence probes for sensing Cr(VI) ions [23]. Fluorescence-based sensing with 1,8-naphthalimide-based probes was also demonstrated for Cr(VI) detection with high specificity [24]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs), the quasi-zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials (size
<
20 nm), have received continued attention due to their facile synthesis, biocompatibility, good water solubility, chemical stability, and chemical engineering tunability of emission/absorption band structures [4,6,25–27]. These advantages make them potential candidates as great fluorescent probes for the quantification of various analytes [4,6,25–32]. Due to the exceptional physiochemical, optical, and electrical properties of CDs, they have been used in a variety of research fields, including photosensitive (as a sensor and for anti-counterfeiting), energy (supercapacitors, photovoltaics, catalysis, and in the form of light-emitting diodes), and capable biomedicine for cancer therapeutics. Additionally, they are known to be inexpensive materials that may be created from a variety of easily accessible chemicals [28–30]. However, few reports exist in which the surface of CDs has been further altered using different ligands to target and identify mitochondria [31–33]. These kinds of functionalized nanoprobes have trouble with multistep synthesis and fluorescence degradation during conjugation, which makes labeling them ineffective. In addition, there are few methods with bright luminescent and bare CDs (particularly without any polymer support) for mitochondrial-targeted labeling in cancer cells [34]. Therefore, simple, stable, one-pot synthesized, and strong luminescent CDs demonstrating effective mitochondria targeting in cancer cells and selective Cr(VI) ions quantification are extremely required.

Herein. we presented the Nitrogen/Sulfur co-doped carbon dots (N/S CDs) as fluorescent probes for sensing Cr(VI) ions. N/S CDs that were one-pot synthesized in a facile way emitted photoluminescence peaked at the wavelength of around 615 nm and its peak showed no shift under different excitation wavelengths, so-called excitation-independent emission. Additionally, the CD's photoluminescence intensity remained pH-independent and stable under high ionic strength such as NaCl. When bringing Cr(VI) ions near N/S CDs in an aqueous solution, their emission was quenched. The photoluminescence quenching resulted from inner filter effects (IFE) and turned out to be sufficiently specific to Cr(VI) sensing under the coexistence of other metal cations/anions that might interfere with it, such as Cr(III). These properties of N/S CDs could lend themselves to specific rapid sensing of Cr(VI) in a wide concentration range, i.e., from 7 to 70 μM. Its limit of detection (LOD) was 53.5 nM, even reflecting capabilities of ultrasensitive sensing.

We also demonstrated the localization of mitochondria of human cancer cells such as ME 180 cells and Hela cells, using N/S CDs as fluorescent nanoprobes. N/S CDs penetrated into cells and labeled the mitochondria of the cells for successful imaging. On top of the above-mentioned excellent sensing ability of Cr(VI) ions, this mitochondria localization feature enabled N/S CDs to be recognized as versatile fluorescent nanoprobes with biocompatibility, finding potential applications extended to sensing of various hazardous toxic ions and imaging of various types of cells.

Methylene blue dye (MB), citric acid (CA), methanol, potassium dichromate, and chromium chloride were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. For the selective sensing tests, metal cations and inorganic anions salts were acquired from Sigma Aldrich in the USA. Analytical reagent grade or above substances and reagents were all utilized directly after purchase without further purification. Without any notification, all solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ cm).

Data on the absorption spectrum were gathered using a single-beam UV–Vis absorption Spectrophotometer (Varian Cary® 50, Agilent, USA) and quartz cuvettes with a 10 mm pathway distance. The fluorescence titration experiments at room temperature were measured with a spectrofluorometer (FS-2, SCINCO, Republic of Korea). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of N/S CDs was taken from an infrared spectrometer (FTIR-4600, JASCO, Japan) via the ATR technique. Fluorescence decay lifetime investigations were executed utilizing a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) device using a 420 nm laser as the excitation light source (Horiba Scientific, Japan). The morphological examinations were conducted on a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) apparatus (TECNAI G2 F30, FEI, USA) with a 300 kV operating voltage. An apparatus (Multilab-2000, Thermo Scientific, USA) with a monochromatic AlK X-ray source (1486.6 eV) for excitation was used to perform the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis.

N/S CDs were prepared by using the following single-step hydrothermal synthetic protocol. In brief, 10 mL of methanol was utilized to liquify 2.5 mg of MB dye and 1.25 g of CA. The mixture was then homogenized using a sonicator for >15 min. The reaction mixture was thereafter put into a 25 mL Teflon autoclave and warmed for 5 h at 180 ± 5 °C. The collected reaction output mixture was then allowed to cool to ambient temperature naturally before being centrifugated for 30 min at a speed of 2800 rcf, which went through a 0.2 μm filter membrane to eliminate the larger particles. Additionally, freeze-drying was used to produce powder samples of N/S CDs, which were then stored at 4 °C for characterization and sensing purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: General procedure for Cr(VI) ions by N/S CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Solution-based Cr(VI) ions chemo sensing was carried out in a completely aqueous media. Briefly, an aqueous dispersion of 0.7 mL of N/S CDs (10 mg/mL) was transferred in a 5 mL calibrated glass tube. Following that, different concentrations of an aqueous medium of Cr(VI) ions were spiked, and the final capacity was made to the mark by mixing ultra-pure water and incubating it for 2 min at room temperature (∼25 °C). Finally, at an excitation wavelength of 418 nm, the reaction mixtures' emission spectrum data at 615 nm were captured. The color variation that was expanded for the smartphone-based color ratio technique is seen in this reaction combination. All emission titration measurements were minimum repeated five times.

The QY of the as-synthesized N/S CDs was calculated using the relative quantum yield determination method. Here, Rhodamine B (Rh) dye solution (QY = 56 %) in ethanol (0.001 M, n 1.36) was utilized as a standard/reference fluorescence indicator. The following equation (Eq. (1)) was used to get the QY of the samples [35,36]:

Whereas, I and A denote the combined emission maximum and absorbance, respectively. n symbolizes the refractive index of the medium. In subscripts of S and R denote the samples and reference materials, respectively. QYR is the QY of the Rh dye solution.

The practical use of the synthesized N/S CDs was investigated on dosing tap and drinking water samples with various dosage levels of Cr(VI) ions. These water samples were obtained at the Gachon University, Bio-Nano Research Institute in Seongnam-si. There is no need for a pretreatment process because the collected water samples are used directly. Using the traditional addition approach, distinctive solutions of Cr(VI) ions at various dosage levels were made and spiked with real water samples. An appropriate amount of Cr(VI) ions spiked natural waters (drinking and tap water) samples were assorted into N/S CDs, and then steady-state emission spectra were recorded in the area of 550–690 nm with an excitation source of 418 nm. The recovery percentage was evaluated using the ratio of spiked and detected Cr(VI) ions (Eq. (2)) [37,38]. Additionally, the experiment was repeated up to five times under identical experimental settings to calculate the relative standard deviation (RSD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mitochondrial targeted imaging and cytotoxic studies
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental protocols of in vitro cytotoxic and mitochondrial-targeted cancer cell imaging studies were given in the supporting information file.

Generally, the hydrothermal method is the simplest and most extensively employed protocol for the production of CDs. Compared to the other synthetic approaches for CDs such as pyrolysis, electrochemical oxidation, arc discharge, microwave-assisted, and acid oxidation methods, this hydrothermal method has superior characteristics that are simple, effective, cost-effective, cost-effective, and biodegradable [39–41]. In light of these features, we have chosen a hydrothermal-based synthetic protocol for the current work. Moreover, through doping with different heteroatoms such as phosphorous (P), N, boron (B), and, S doping, CDs may attain new surface conditions and unusual luminous characteristics, like thermal or pH responsivity and higher QY [39,42,43]. In particular, N/S CDs have garnered increased interest because an S element has lesser electronegativity and takes a collaborative impact with an N atom [40,41]. Moreover, formulating N/S CDs with dye molecule-based raw materials is relatively rare. Thus, we can select a suitable carbon source with identifiable heteroatoms to generate N/S CDs with improved qualities. In this respect, CA and MB dye moieties were elected as the precursors to produce N/S CDs by solvothermal method at 180 °C in 5 h (Scheme 1 ). The next step was to characterize N/S CDs using various spectroscopic analyses and HR-TEM pictures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of N/S CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Using absorbance and luminescence spectroscopy investigations, the photo physio-chemical characteristics of as-prepared N/S CDs were examined. The typical outcomes are demonstrated in Fig. 1 . One peak at ∼283 nm in the range of high energy area can be seen in Fig. 1a and is attributable to the π- π* transition in sp2 domains of an aromatic conjugated system. In the meantime, three peaks were noted in the lower energy region (400−600 nm) at ∼412, ∼470, and ∼525 nm, which are formed from the aromatic sp2 bonds of the n−π* transitions (Fig. 1a) [39,44]. N/S CDs display wine red color under daylight, whereas they emit bright orange color fluorescence color under UV light (λ = 365 nm). The strong emission maximum at 615 nm under the illumination at 418 nm that these N/S CDs exhibit, as visualized in Fig. 1b, amplifies the extremely fluorescent nature of the N/S CDs [42]. Additionally, using Rh (58 %) as the emission standard via reference approach, the QY of N/S CDs was determined (from Eq. (1)) as being approximately 16 %.

Furthermore, CDs fluorescence stabilities should be considered as a key aspect for any potential applications. The impact of UV light treatment time on the emissive nature of N/S CDs was established in this study to support the ability to continuously reflect. As noted in Fig. S1 (a & b), the emissive nature of N/S CDs remains constant even after the sample has been continuously exposed to radiation for 60 min. Furthermore, we have tested the emissive nature of N/S CDs below some environmental settings such as various pH, and ionic conditions, and corresponding outcomes were displayed in Figs. S2 and S3. The outstanding colloidal stability of N/S CDs is exemplified in Fig. S2 (a & b), there is no fluorescence intensity variation at 615 nm even when the NaCl and KCl contents are increased to 0.1 mM. Along with being resistant to the salt effect, N/S CDs are also impervious to pH changes. Fig. S3 demonstrates that there is barely any variation in fluorescence intensity over the pH range of 3.0 to 10.0. These findings suggest that N/S CDs have strong photostability and colloidal stability, which is advantageous for real-world applications like environmental water sample analysis and bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and chemical composition investigations of N/S CDs
ce:section.ce:para: HR-TEM pictures were utilized to confirm the morphology, size, and structure of the N/S CDs. Fig. 2 illustrates HR-TEM images of CDs that are doped with N and S. As visualized in Fig. 2, the size of the N/S CDs in their as-prepared state is spherical in shape and well dispersed throughout a range of 2 to 4.5 nm, with a typical size of around ∼3 ± 1.5 nm (minimum 100 particles taken for analysis). The majority of N/S CDs are seen to have a visible lattice assembly by the high-resolution TEM (Fig. 2), proving the crystalline nature of the CDs [4].

The optical features of N/S CDs are fluently influenced by their surface chemical states and structure [39]. Hence, FT-IR, and XPS studies were accomplished to get the chemical structural data of N/S CDs. Fig. S4 displays the infrared spectrum of N/S CDs. The IR frequency at 2940 cm-1 corresponded to C − H stretching vibration. The existence of carboxyl and other oxygen-holding functional groups can be established by the IR bands at 1736, 1205, and 1447 cm-1, which could support the presence of functional groups such as C = O, C − O, and C = C. [45,46]. As depicted in Fig. S4, the IR absorption bands at around 3388 and 3006 cm-1 in the IR spectrum can be allotted to O − H and N − H stretching vibrations, signifying the occurrence of the hydroxyl and amino functional groups. The remarkable solubility characteristics of N/S CDs in water are provided by these hydrophilic functional groups. The IR stretching frequencies around 1087 and 1446 cm-1 are related to the C − S and C − N, demonstrating the effective doping of S and N heteroatoms in the chemical composition of N/S CDs [45].

Furthermore, an XPS investigation was also executed to check the surface elemental conformation of N/S CDs, and the collected survey spectrum was displayed in Fig. S5. As illustrated in Fig. S5, four typical peaks in the XPS survey spectrum can be noted at 166.26, 285.22, 402.30, and, 532.01, eV which stand for S 2p, C 1 s, N 1 s, and, O 1 s, respectively. This finding indicates that the primary elements present in N/S CDs are S, C, N, and O [45,47]. The deconvoluted XPS spectra of separate elements containing the N/S CDs were subsequently analyzed and represented in Fig. 3 . More specifically, the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of C 1 s can be close-fitting to three similar deconvoluted characteristic peaks placed at 284.65, 286.94, and 289.05 eV, assigning to the C = C/C − C (sp2 carbon), C − S/C − N/C − O, and C = O bonds (sp3 carbon), respectively (Fig. 3a) [39,48]. Regarding the O 1 s deconvoluted XPS spectrum, there are two characteristic peaks with respective centers at 530.58, 532.10, and 533.63 eV that show the presence of C − O of and C = O bonds (Fig. 3b). The XPS spectrum of N 1 s is deconvoluted to the main peak centered at 400.15 eV, which was linked with the C − N/N − H bonds (Fig. 3c) [48]. In the meantime, the S 2p XPS spectrum was deconvoluted into two major peaks at 164.06 and 166.17 eV for the C − S bond (S2p3/2 and S2p1/2) of phenothiazine S [45,49] (Fig. 3d). The findings illustrate that the synthesized N/S CDs feature hydrophilic functional groups, which may assist in increasing the solubility of CDs in a water medium. These XPS and FT-IR outcomes are in good accordance with each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of Cr(VI) ions based on N/S CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, after successful characterization of as-synthesized N/S CDs were employed in the area of chemosensors. In order to achieve extremely sensitive chemosensing of Cr(VI) ions, the reaction of analyte solution on the pH-dependent fluorescence of N/S CDs was examined. The fluorescence reactions were autonomously assessed a minimum thrice for every pH level, and a mean pH value was estimated. As produced, N/S CDs are extremely stable in the pH ranges of 3 to 10. (Fig. S3). However, the pH of the reaction conditions was conducted at ∼7 to maintain the ambient conditions in our work. Furthermore, investigating the effects of incubation time helped optimize the analytical process. A constant emission maximum at 615 nm can be achieved after 2 min of reaction time with the introduction of 10 μM of Cr(VI) ions to N/S CDs. The emission maximum wavelength of N/S CDs is stable for at least 60 min and exhibits strong photobleaching resistance. Thus, 2 min interaction period is sufficient for the fluorescence titration measurements (Fig. S6).

Under optimized conditions, the steady-state emission titration experiments of N/S CDs with incremental amounts (7 to 70 μM) of Cr(VI) ions were monitored and a representative collected process was given in Fig. 4 . Before mixing the Cr(VI) ions, N/S CDs display the fluorescence band at 615 nm and a bright orange color upon the treatment of UV light (λ = 365 nm). The N/S CDs possess exceptional emissive properties were used as a sensing signal to find Cr(VI) ions. When Cr(VI) ion concentrations are raised, there is a noticeable quenching in emission maxima as well as a slight bathochromic shift of the emission band, which confirms the suitability of Cr(VI) as a fluorescent “turn-off” probe (Fig. 4a). In the meanwhile, following the introduction of Cr(VI) ions, N/S CDs reacted with them resulting in the mixed solution gradually changing its color from wine red to yellow. The emissive color of the colloidal dispersion of N/S CDs with Cr(VI) ions also changed from a bright orange color to a dark non-fluorescent color under the UV lamp (λ = 365 nm) (inset of Fig. 4a (i and ii)). The emission peak intensities were also redshifted from 616 to 625 nm, further supporting the idea that Cr(VI) and N/S CDs mix to generate a ground-state complex [50]. Moreover, QY also reduces from 16 % to 3.6 % for N/S CDs after the final spiking of Cr(VI) ions revealing the efficient emission quenching. The observed notable emission quenching data were involved in determining the quantity of Cr(VI) ions in water samples. The calibration plot was drawn between various quantities of Cr(VI) ions and the emission intensities and is viewed in Fig. 4b. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the calibration curve illustrates the linear range for Cr(VI) ions is 7–70 μM with linear regression equation of y = 41.35 – 3252.35 [Cr(VI) ions] μM (R2 = 0.9873, n = 5). The lowest limit of detection (LOD) of the present N/S CDs-based nanoprobe for Cr(VI) ions was 53.5 nM, which was appraised from the method of LOD =3σ/K [37,38] (σ stands for the blank signal standard deviation (n = 5) and K represents the calibration curve slope). The calculated LOD value (53.5 nM) of the current work is much smaller than the tolerable dose of Cr(VI) ions in drinking water (10 μM) approved by the WHO. The shown N/S CDs-based nanoprobe is also more efficient in sensitive chemosensing of Cr(VI) ions in drinking water samples. The obtained analytical characteristics from this study, including LOD and linear range, were equated to previously published C-based optical determination of Cr(VI) ions and were summarized in Table S1. The demonstrated N/S CDs-based chemosensor unveiled well or equal sensitivity when compared to previously reported carbon-based fluorescence sensors [1,4,7,50–63] (Table S1) and could be used to quantify Cr(VI) in various environmental matrixes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Plausible sensing pathway of N/S CDs with Cr(VI) ions
ce:section.ce:para: A quenching response on the fluorescence maximum of N/S CDs could be competent to measure the levels of Cr(VI) ions. Fluorescence quenching typically occurs through different mechanisms, including dynamic/static quenching processes, excited state charge transfer processes, IFE, and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) processes. To verify this, the collected emission quenching data were further applied to the classical Stern-Volmer equation [64,65] as F0/F = 1 + KSV [Cr(VI) ions], whereas F0 and F are the emission maximum of blank aqueous dispersion of N/S CDs and N/S CDs with Cr(VI) ions, respectively. Q is the Cr(VI) ions's concentration, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer constant. Stern-Volmer plots of N/S CDs with Cr(VI) ions were given in Figs. S7 and 5 a. The Stern-Volmer plot of Cr(VI) ions-induced emission quenching displays upward-curving bends rather than a linear correlation, as shown in Fig. S7. Such a nonlinear Stern-Volmer plot is attributed to the emission destruction in association with ground state complex formation and dynamic quenching [64]. In addition, there are many rigorous interactions between N/S CDs, and higher quantities of Cr(VI) ions.

The excellent linearity in the concentration range between 7 and 56 μM is shown by the Stern-Volmer plot in Fig. 5a, which demonstrates the static quenching involved between the interaction of N/S CDs, and analyte. From the slope of this linear plot, KSV is estimated as 4.87 × 104 L/mol. The remarkably reduced fluorescence intensities of Cr(VI) ions towards N/S CDs are amply demonstrated by this estimated KSV value. The IFE/FRET mechanism is brought about by a spectral intersection between the fluorophore's exciting/emissive peak and the analyte's absorption band in the detecting system [50,51]. Therefore, it is essential to carefully choose the optimal absorber and donor pairings to produce highly effective IFE/FRET-based fluorescent chemosensors. However, a direct chemical interaction between the analytes (accepter) and the fluorophore (donor) is required for other photophysical processes [56,57].

To check the IFE/FRET process involved in our study, the spectral overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Cr(VI) ions and the emission/excitation spectrum of N/S CDs and visualized in Fig. 5b. As exemplified in Fig. 5b, Cr(VI) ions display absorption peaks at 266, 354, and 441 nm viewing good spectral touching of excitation (418 nm) and emission band at 615 nm of N, S-CDs. Therefore, Cr(VI) ions not only absorb the emission light from N/S CDs but also block the excitation light of N/S CDs. The spectral overlapping between the donor (excitation/emission) and accepter (absorbance) satisfies the IFE/FRET process condition. To distinguish the IFE/FRET process, time-resolved fluorescence experiments for N/S CDs with and without Cr(VI) ions were performed and corresponding results were presented in Fig. 6 . As established in Fig. 6, the fluorescence decay lifetime value for N/S CDs is 3.36 ns according to the bi-exponential function fitting of the fluorescence decay data. There has been very little change (from 3.36 to 3.33 ns) in the fluorescence lifetime of N/S CDs when Cr(VI) ions are generated, (Fig. 6), which clearly illustrates the non-fluorescent complex formation or static quenching process involved between the interaction of nanoprobe and analyte. The possibility of the FRET process between Cr(VI) ions and N/S CDs was excluded by fluorescence lifetime observations. As a result, it is conceived that the combined effect of static quenching and the IFE process are the key mechanisms diminishing the emission intensity of N/S CDs by Cr(VI) ions sensing (Fig. 5 c). The IFE plays a role as a mechanism for a practical sensing device [66–68]. Since there is no requirement for a chemical connection between the analyte and the fluorophore, IFE is considered a more straightforward and adaptable type of interaction in fluorometry [66–68]. The environmental industry can gain from employing the recently developed N/S CDs -based "turn-off" fluorescent label for the quantification of Cr(VI) ions using the IFE technique because of its noticeable merits of effortlessness, suitability, and speed of execution.

To confirm the impact of other metallic ions on Cr(VI) binding ability to N/S CDs, fluorescence spectral and colorimetric analysis was performed in the incidence of numerous potential interfering metal ions such as Ag(I), Hg(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Fe(II), Ca(II), Al(III), Al(III), Co(III), Cr(III), and, Fe(III) at 5-fold higher concentration than Cr(VI) ions. The collected fluorescence spectral and colorimetric changes are displayed in Fig. 7 . Fig. 7 illustrated that upon the addition of potentials interfering metal ions including Cr(VI), Ag(I), Hg(II), Pb(II), Cd(II), Zn(II), Ni(II), Cu(II), Fe(II), Ca(II), Al(III), Au(III), Co(III), Cr(III), and, Fe(III) into N/S CDs, Cr(VI) ions only leads to the notable alteration in the fluorescence signal (fluorescence color and spectrum), while the emission quenching effect concerned by other metal ions are nearly negligible. At the same time, except for Cr(VI) ions other potentially interfering metal ions did not alter the bright orange emissive color of N/S CDs, which was visualized naked eye under UV light (inset of Fig. 7). The reason for the high specificity is that Cr(VI) ions exhibit absorption maxima at 266, 354, and 441 nm, which are well spectrally overlapping with the excitation spectrum of N/S CDs (418 nm), but other potentially interfering metal ions do not show an absorption peak in the same region. It is believed that there is neither a static/non-fluorescent complex formation nor an IFE procedure between N/S CDs, and Cr(VI) ions, which accounts for the excellent discrimination of N/S CDs towards Cr(VI) ions. The interference studies demonstrate the better specificity of the N/S CDs-based fluorescence sensor towards Cr(VI) ions over other environmentally relevant metal ions, further indicating its potential use for environmental water samples.

Identifying Cr(VI) ions in environmental water samples allowed researchers to examine the viability of the suggested approach for real-world applications. A standard adding strategy was utilized to reduce the potential matrix effect of several interference species. The created sensor's capabilities were tested by first adding Cr(VI) ions to the pre-treated water samples in a range of concentrations using the conventional addition process. Cr(VI) ions spiked environmental water samples were spiked into N/S CDs, and emission spectral studies were performed. Based on the emission quenching response of N/S CDs in two separate water samples, namely tap and drinking water samples, the recovery quantity of Cr(VI) ions was estimated. The outcomes shown in Table S2 demonstrate that the detected concentration using the proposed nanosensor is adjacent to the spiked quantities of Cr(VI) ions, and the recoveries amount in the range of 92.30–96.75 % of environmental water samples, demonstrating that very little interference prevents the fast identification of trace Cr(VI) ion concentrations in the realistic water samples. Real water sample results from the proposed fluorescence sensing technique are in good agreement with data from atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS). (Table S3). It is important to mention that the measured relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3 % showing that fluorescence sensors based on N/S CDs can measure Cr(VI) ions in environmental samples.

The assessment of biocompatibility is the most important focus of cytotoxicity testing. CDs are created entirely using environmentally benign processes and harmless ingredients, it is quite likely that they are biodegradable nanoparticles [69]. In the beginning, we assess these N/S CDs' cytotoxicity toward normal cells. It is crucial to ascertain early on that these nanoparticles' safety in normal cells is ensured since their intended uses include aiding in the diagnosis of cancer cells and the delivery of medications to tumor cells. N/S CDs did not display any substantial harmful effect in the normal cells which suggested that they are the most safe and appropriate for this kind of utilization. For this, the cytotoxicity of these N/S CDs was established against the four different human cancer cells such as Hela cells, cervical cancer cells (ME 180 cells and CaSki cells), and SiHa cells by MTT protocol. MTT cytotoxicity outcomes are displayed in Fig. S8. Fig. S8 presents that 90 % of the cells were still alive after being exposed to N/S CDs at concentrations between 0.5 and 25 µg/mL. However, at higher concentrations of N/S CDs, such as 50–100 µg/mL, there was some minimal cytotoxicity. Furthermore, under physiological settings, N/S CD-treated Hela cells, ME 180 cells, CaSki cells, and SiHa cells produced healthy cell morphology (Figs. 8 , S8–S11). More investigation is needed to completely understand the N/S CDs' cytotoxic effect on Hela cells, ME 180 cells, CaSki cells, and SiHa cells. It is exactly unclear how the N/S CDs work on these selected cancer cells. Moreover, the efficiency of N/S CDs may differ reliant on the cell line type utilized and the raw materials of the N/S CDs [69]. Overall, the current findings using N/S CDs at low doses (100 μg/mL) demonstrated lower cytotoxicity and acceptable biocompatibility. Given these findings, N/S CDs appear to be a suitable nano-platform for functions associated with biology and bioimaging.

To assess N/S CDs' capability for focusing on mitochondria, Hela cells, and ME 180 cells were employed to conduct the mitochondrial localization investigation. Mitotracker Red, a commercial dye, was used to label cancer cells to explore the ability of N/S CDs to penetrate the mitochondria. Initially, Hela and ME 180 cancer cells were treated with 50 μg/mL N/S CDs for 30 min. Afterward, the N/S CDs treated cancer cells were further stained with MitoTracker Red (0.390 μM) for ∼30–40 min, and a confocal fluorescence microscope was employed to capture the resultant fluorescence signals. Fig. 9 depicts the collected immunofluorescence microscopic illustrations. It can be noticed from Fig. 9 that the findings designate that the pictures of the green fluorescence of N/S CDs are well-colocalized with the images of the red fluorescence of MitoTracker Red in both cell lines. Furthermore, the ImageJ software package estimates Pearson's colocalization (R) and overlap coefficient (Rr) values in Fig. 8e and 8k. The estimated R and Rr values are 0.8133 and 0.9318 for Hela cells and 0.9174 and 0.9744 for ME 180 cancer cells. The selected Hela cells and ME 180 cells were stably labeled with N/S CDs, indicating that they had an outstanding aptitude to target mitochondria in cancer cells [70–73]. The efficient detection and labeling of mitochondria are facilitated by the structural characteristics of MB dye molecules that are present on the surface of N/S-CDs. Furthermore, the cationic MB dye exhibits remarkable penetration into the negatively charged mitochondrial intermembrane potential barrier due to its polarity and high lipophilicity [30,34]. In addition, N/S CDs exhibit less than 3 nm in size, which easily penetrates the mitochondrial cell membrane. With the benefits of wash-free and long-term imaging, N/S CDs have an exceptional ability to target mitochondria in cancer cells. This shows great aptitude for mitochondrial monitoring and mitochondrial-based drug management and therapy.

Bright orange color emissive N, S-CDs have been synthesized by a low-cost hydrothermal reaction using CA as a carbon source and MB dye as an N/S heteroatom source. The N/S CDs emitted photoluminescence peaked at 615 nm under excitation light at 418 nm. The fluorescence “Turn-off” of N/S CDs was demonstrated for the detection of Cr(VI) due to fluorescence quenching induced by inner filter effects that involved non-fluorescent complex bound states of Cr(VI) ions and N/S CDs. We found that the strategy of the N/S CDs-based fluorescence quenching offered the reliable rapid sensing of Cr(VI) ions in the detection range of 7–70 μM (R2 = 0.9873) with LOD of 53.5 nM (much smaller than WHO standard). This strategy was also demonstrated with drinking water while the sensing accuracy turned out to be sufficient. More interestingly, the fluorescence quenching was accompanied by the visual color change of the solution that included N/S CDs and Cr(VI) ions. This could inspire the use of a color-sensitive detection of a smartphone device for Cr(VI) ions in future applications. Moreover, we also found that N/S CDs could be utilized as effective fluorescent nanoprobes for labeling mitochondria of cancer cells, leading to their successful localization within the cells for cell imaging.

In conclusion, the N/S CDs fluorescence quenching strategies presented in this work may open a door for sensitive and cost-effective monitoring of Cr(VI) ions in water to preserve the ecosystem and human health and for mitochondria-based cell imaging. Possible applications of N/S CDs could be found in sensing various hazardous ions in water and biomedical imaging and therapy.

Ramar Rajamanikandan: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. D.S. Prabakaran: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Kandasamy Sasikumar: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Joo Seon Seok: Data curation, Formal analysis. Gyeongchan Lee: Data curation, Formal analysis. Heongkyu Ju: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
